title: David Boies, Zack Schiller's CenterStage to Create AI Personalized Stories for Kids
text:
Oct 9, 2025 6:30am PT
Film Producers David Boies, Zack Schiller Launch AI Start-Up CenterStage, Which Will Let Kids Star in Their Own Stories
Founders say they have inked IP deals with PBS and Pete the Cat, with more on the way
By
Todd Spangler
Plus Icon
Todd Spangler
NY Digital Editor
xpangler
Latest
Spotify Debuts New Logo: A Disco Ball to Celebrate Its ‘Party of the Year(s)’
35 minutes ago

22 hours ago

Courtesy of CenterStage Technologies
Will AI unleash an entirely new wave of entertainment — starring
you
?
That’s the premise of
CenterStage Technologies
, formed by
David Boies
and
Zack Schiller
as an offshoot of their film financing and production company Boies Schiller Entertainment. CenterStage plans to launch its first product later this fall: an AI-powered, personalized storytelling platform aimed at children, letting them create “kid-safe stories” in adventures featuring popular characters. The company says it has inked intellectual property deals with PBS and
children’s book franchise Pete the Cat
— and, according to Schiller, it’s in discussions about IP licensing pacts with a major Hollywood studio and other family-oriented brands.
Related Stories
Sandra Hüller Soaks Up 5-Minute Standing Ovation for 'Fatherland' as Oscar Race Starts Early in Cannes
Ukrainian War Thriller 'Honeymoon' Wins Mediawan, BYD Backed Build Your Dream Prize in Cannes
“Our purpose at CenterStage is to allow personalization at scale, and let the consumer star in and direct their own productions,” says Schiller.
Popular on Variety
Generative AI has been a
major flashpoint in Hollywood
. Actors, writers and other creatives have been concerned the tech will erode their livelihoods, while studios have been actively pursuing copyright infringement litigation against some AI players they allege have ripped off their IP. This week, amid a proliferation of videos created with OpenAI’s Sora 2 that feature recognizable movie and TV characters, the
Motion Picture Association called on the company to take “immediate action” to fix its copyright opt-out system
.
CenterStage’s principals recognize the hot-button AI issues in the industry. Boies, a high-profile attorney, says a key part of CenterStage’s approach is that it will empower IP owners to safely and ethically develop personalized narratives using AI. The company says its characters, concepts and stories are “completely protected” with “highly controlled” training environments, recursive safety protocols, and reinforcement learning with human and AI feedback.
CenterStage “is committed to respecting the intellectual property rights of others and to protecting its own intellectual property,” Boies says. And its AI system has guardrails in place to ensure kid-safe content, he says: “We don’t want Pete the Cat to become Felix the Cat.”
Meanwhile, according to Schiller, CenterStage is using WGA writers to create its prompts and has tapped Hollywood artists to create its own versions of public-domain characters. “We think we are creating more opportunities, not taking them away,” he says. “We are a human-first AI company.”
In this new era in entertainment, “technology amplifies human creativity to deliver stories that resonate deeply and responsibly,” Schiller says. Boies and Schiller have been working quietly on developing CenterStage over the last year and a half. “We saw a coming wave of technology that will need to adapt to the economics of the movie space,” Schiller says.
CenterStage’s two other co-founders are George Cawood, managing partner of entertainment marketing and production agency Framework Studio, who is leading platform development; and data scientist Zank Bennett, CEO and founder of Bennett Data Science, who is heading up the AI team. CenterStage is in the middle of raising a round of funding from outside investors; Schiller declined to name investors at this point.
Currently, CenterStage has about 30 employees. It is largely based at the Culver City offices of Boies Schiller Entertainment. One reason Schiller and Boies think CenterStage can gain traction with Hollywood partners is because they’ve been in the business of making movies since 2012. Films co-financed and produced by BSE include “The Babysitter,” directed by McG; “Escape Plan,” starring Arnold Schwarzenegger and Sylvester Stallone; “Jane Got a Gun” starring Natalie Portman, Joel Edgerton and Ewan McGregor; and “The Upside,” directed by Neil Burger and starring Kevin Hart, Bryan Cranston and Nicole Kidman.
The genesis of the idea for CenterStage came when Schiller’s son, who is now 8, asked why his dad had never made a movie for him. So Schiller worked with Cawood to make a short film using AI that starred his son as a vet-ninja (veterinarian by day, ninja by night). Schiller realized there was a market for an AI-based platform that could do the same thing at scale. “The first iteration of CenterStage is for children to help them in early reading adoption. It’s educational and entertainment,” he says. Eventually, according to Schiller, “our platform will let people create shows, movies, books and more from whole cloth.”
Schiller says CenterStage is working with multiple AI companies’ large language models (LLMs), which it has trained and fine-tuned using its own proprietary visual model. “Everything we train on is protected — it can’t get out into the wild,” he says. “Anything a studio gives us cannot leak out into the larger knowledge base.”
Who are CenterStage’s competitors? Boies says it’s “everybody and nobody. Nobody is doing what we are doing. On the other hand, we are competing with everybody who is trying to get the attention and participation of consumers.” CenterStage’s difference, he says, is that in its model users are not purely spectators: “They are able to create their own environment.”
Adds Schiller, “It’s a new product that doesn’t exist. We are creating the rules of the road as we go.”
Pictured top: David Boies (left), Zack Schiller
